91: **Brazil**, level on 7 points with one other team from their group (position not yet separated, GD +6), face **Norway**, 2nd in **Group I** on 6 points, at MetLife Stadium in East Rutherford at 1:00 PM PT — a stadium that will host just one more match, the final on July 19. Ancelotti arrives without Raphinha (thigh, sustained vs Haiti) and Lucas Paquetá (thigh/hamstring, forced off at half-time vs Japan), while Casemiro also walked off late in that 2-1 win, leaving Bruno Guimarães — who leads the tournament with four assists, more than any Brazil player at a single edition since Pelé's six in 1970 — as the fulcrum of a reshaped midfield. Norway, whose 2-1 win over Ivory Coast via Haaland's 86th-minute tap-in was their first-ever knockout-stage victory at the finals, have never lost to Brazil across four meetings (Norway W2, D2), including Kjetil Rekdal's winning penalty in Marseille in 1998; Haaland has scored in each of his last 13 competitive internationals and needs one more goal to become only the eighth European to score in his first four tournament appearances, with Julian Ryerson (thigh, off after 13 minutes vs Senegal) still a doubt at right-back. The winner meets the **Mexico**–**England** victor in the quarter-finals.
92: **Mexico**, 1st in **Group A** on 9 points and the only remaining side not to have conceded a single goal across five tournament matches, host **England**, 1st in **Group L** on 7 points, at the Estadio Azteca at 5:00 PM PT — a venue sitting 2,240 metres above sea level where Mexico are unbeaten in 10 all-time tournament matches (W8, D2), a record no other nation holds at a single stadium across three host editions. Mexico beat Ecuador 2-0 in the Round of 32 — Julián Quiñones scoring his third goal of the tournament before teeing up Raúl Jiménez — while 17-year-old Gilberto Mora, potentially the second-youngest player to start a knockout match in tournament history (20 days older than Pelé in 1958), came through unscathed; England's 2-1 comeback win over DR Congo was sealed by Harry Kane's 75th-minute equaliser and winner, taking him to 13 all-time tournament goals and past Pelé's career total of 12, though Reece James (hamstring) and Jarell Quansah (ankle) remain out, leaving the right-back position unresolved. The only competitive meeting between these sides ended England 2-0 Mexico in the 1966 group stage at Wembley; the winner here advances to the quarter-finals to face **Brazil** or **Norway**.
90: **Canada**, level on 4 points with one other team from their group (position not yet separated, GD +5), face **Morocco**, level on 7 points with one other team from their group (position not yet separated, GD +3), at 10:00 AM PT at NRG Stadium in Houston — Canada's first-ever Round of 16 match. Canada beat South Africa 1-0 in the Round of 32 thanks to Stephen Eustaquio's injury-time volley, but arrive without Ismael Kone (broken leg, vs Qatar) and with Alphonso Davies — who has not started a minute this tournament following a hamstring recurrence — potentially making his first start since March 2025. Morocco eliminated the Netherlands on penalties in the Round of 32, with Issa Diop's 91st-minute equaliser forcing extra time before a 3-2 shootout victory; Ismael Saibari, whose move to Bayern Munich was confirmed the day after that win, scored three goals in the group stage and leads their attack, though centre-back Chadi Riad (substituted in the 75th minute vs the Netherlands) is questionable. The winner advances to the quarter-finals in Boston to face **France** or **Paraguay**.
89: **Paraguay**, level on 4 points with one other team from their group (position not yet separated, GD -2), face **France**, 1st in **Group I** on 9 points, at 2:00 PM PT at Lincoln Financial Field in Philadelphia. Paraguay beat Germany 4-3 on penalties after a 1-1 draw in the Round of 32 — Julio Enciso scoring the goal, Orlando Gill the shootout hero — but arrive with centre-back Omar Alderete out (knee, sustained in the group-stage finale), while Enciso and Gabriel Avalos are both carrying knocks and being monitored; Diego Gomez returns from suspension in midfield. France have become the first team in tournament history to score 3+ goals in five consecutive matches — 13 total across four matches at this stage — with Mbappe on six goals (level with Messi in the Golden Boot race) and Olise providing five assists, the most by any player at a single tournament since 1994; **Paraguay** have failed to beat **France** in five previous attempts (W0 D2 L3), losing 1-0 in extra time in the 1998 last-16. The winner faces the **Canada**–**Morocco** winner in the quarter-finals in Boston.
